skill.name: stripe-agent
skill.description: Guides Stripe billing operations for Unite-Hub, including product and price setup, checkout sessions, subscriptions, webhooks, and test/live billing separation.
skill.summary: Manage Stripe billing workflows with test and live mode separation for Unite-Hub.
skill.author: CleanExpo

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many backticks as shell execution, plus hardcoded URLs, secret-key names, weak crypto, reconnaissance, and a dangerous-combination heuristic. Review found the execution, crypto, and reconnaissance signals are Markdown documentation false positives, while the real concern is legitimate but sensitive Stripe billing control. Publish only with clear warnings, scoped Stripe credentials, and human approval for live-mode changes.

medium_findings.title: Stripe Billing Mutations Through MCP
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to create products, prices, checkout sessions, customers, invoices, and subscription updates through Stripe MCP calls. This is legitimate for a billing skill, but mistakes can affect customer billing and live subscription state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The listed MCP operations are explicit and directly modify Stripe objects. The context is legitimate billing administration, so this is an operational risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Credential-Dependent Live Billing Mode
medium_findings.description: The skill documents required Stripe test and live secret keys and webhook secrets. It does not expose real secret values, but it assumes access to credentials that can control payment workflows.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable names are placeholders, not leaked credentials. Confidence is high that sensitive access is required because both test and live secret key names are documented.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified As Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static shell-execution findings mostly point to Markdown code fences, inline identifiers, JSON examples, TypeScript examples, and table entries. I did not find Ruby backtick execution in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are fenced documentation examples and do not execute by themselves. The file contains Markdown instructions rather than Ruby source code.

low_findings.title: Documented CLI Commands Require Operator Control
low_findings.description: The skill includes Stripe CLI and npm command examples for webhook testing and billing scripts. They are manual operational commands, not automatically executed by this file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are real external commands, but they appear in Markdown examples. Risk depends on whether an agent or operator runs them with live credentials.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Redirect And Webhook Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings are checkout redirect URLs and placeholder webhook endpoints. I did not find evidence that these URLs exfiltrate credentials or send data to an unrelated domain.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are shown in Stripe checkout and webhook configuration contexts. They are expected network destinations for this billing workflow.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto And Reconnaissance Signals Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography, system reconnaissance, and network reconnaissance findings do not match the semantic content of the cited lines. I found billing descriptions, webhook events, and error handling text instead.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found no cryptographic algorithm or reconnaissance command. The scanner appears to have matched unrelated words inside normal documentation.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Payment API Mutation Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill describes creating and updating Stripe resources that can affect prices, checkout sessions, invoices, and customer subscriptions. These actions should be gated by explicit user approval and Stripe role scoping.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The MCP call names and parameters show direct Stripe resource creation. The pattern is dangerous only because payment state is sensitive, not because malicious intent is evident.

dangerous_patterns.title: Live Credential Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill separates test and live billing modes using Stripe secret keys and webhook secrets. Any deployment should prevent accidental live-mode use during testing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The test/live split and secret names are explicitly documented. There is no secret leakage, but the workflow can affect real payments when live credentials are available.

content.value_statement: Stripe billing setup is complex when test and live customers must stay separate. This skill guides product setup, checkout, subscription handling, webhooks, and billing audits for Unite-Hub.
actual_capabilities: Defines Stripe product and price setup for Starter, Professional, and Elite tiers.
Guides checkout session creation with customer email, price, workspace metadata, and redirect URLs.
Documents subscription upgrade, downgrade, cancellation, and billing adjustment workflows.
Explains webhook handling for checkout, subscription, invoice paid, and payment failed events.
Describes test and live billing separation using staff roles, domains, and Stripe environment variables.
Provides billing report and configuration audit workflows for products, prices, webhooks, and environment variables.
limitations: The skill is tailored to Unite-Hub pricing, domains, routes, and database fields.
It requires a configured Stripe MCP server or equivalent Stripe tooling.
It documents required environment variables but does not provision secure secret storage.
Live billing actions can affect real customers and should require human approval.

use_cases.title: Launch SaaS billing tiers
use_cases.description: Create matching Stripe products and monthly or annual prices for the Unite-Hub subscription tiers.

use_cases.title: Maintain subscription operations
use_cases.description: Handle checkout, upgrades, cancellations, invoice status, and webhook-driven subscription sync.

use_cases.title: Validate Stripe configuration
use_cases.description: Audit environment variables, products, prices, webhook endpoints, and test/live parity before release.

prompt_templates.title: Audit setup health
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check before using Stripe billing flows.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit our Stripe billing setup for test and live mode. Report missing environment variables, products, prices, and webhooks without making changes.

prompt_templates.title: Create a checkout plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a checkout session for a Professional monthly subscription. Include customer lookup, price selection, metadata, success URL, and cancellation URL.

prompt_templates.title: Handle an upgrade
prompt_templates.scenario: Move a customer from one tier to another.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this subscription upgrade request from Starter to Professional. Explain the Stripe steps, proration handling, and database sync needed.

prompt_templates.title: Review live billing readiness
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced release review for production billing.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare test and live Stripe configuration for release readiness. Identify drift, risky permissions, webhook gaps, and approvals needed before live changes.

output_examples.input: Audit Stripe setup for production readiness.
output_examples.output: The audit reports whether required keys, products, prices, and webhook endpoints are present. It flags missing live webhooks and recommends fixes.

output_examples.input: Prepare checkout for a Professional annual customer.
output_examples.output: The response outlines customer lookup, annual price selection, checkout metadata, redirect behavior, and the approval needed before creating a live session.
The report summarizes revenue, active subscriptions, new customers, churn, monthly recurring revenue, trial conversions, and follow-up actions.

best_practices: Use restricted Stripe keys and separate accounts or roles for test and live work.
Require explicit approval before creating, updating, or canceling live subscriptions.
Run configuration audits before release and after each pricing or webhook change.
anti_patterns: Do not run live billing commands while testing staff or sandbox flows.
Do not store Stripe secret keys in prompts, tickets, logs, or generated documentation.
Do not change prices or subscriptions without recording the customer, workspace, and reason.

faq.question: Does this skill include real Stripe credentials?
faq.answer: No. It lists environment variable names and placeholder values, but deployments must provide secrets securely.

faq.question: Can it make live billing changes?
faq.answer: Yes, when connected to Stripe tooling with live credentials. Live changes should require explicit human approval.

faq.question: Is it specific to Unite-Hub?
faq.answer: Yes. The tiers, routes, domains, metadata, and database sync examples are written for Unite-Hub.

faq.question: Does it handle Stripe webhooks?
faq.answer: It documents webhook events for checkout, subscriptions, invoices, failed payments, and local testing.

faq.question: Can it audit billing configuration?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes checks for environment variables, products, prices, webhooks, and test/live parity.

faq.question: What tools are required?
faq.answer: It expects Stripe MCP access or equivalent Stripe CLI and API tooling configured outside the skill.
